In the quaint town of Chachoengsao, nestled between rolling hills and picturesque landscapes, stood Datdaruni School, known for fostering young minds with a passion for science and curiosity. Three friends, Phoon, Kam, and Lita, were students at this school, united not only by their love for learning but also by their adventurous spirits.

One sunny morning, as the girls gathered around the school's lab table, their teacher, Mr. Noparat, entered with a grave expression. He explained that strange occurrences had been happening around the world — unusual lights in the sky, inexplicable technology glitches, and unsettling rumors. It was as if the planet was bracing for an invasion from beyond the stars.

Phoon, with her keen interest in astronomy, had been tracking these anomalies. Kam, always fascinated by technology, had been deciphering the strange signals emanating from the phenomena. Lita, the master of chemistry, was experimenting with various compounds to decipher the origin of these peculiar occurrences.

One evening, as the girls were engrossed in their studies, a bright light burst forth from the night sky. A colossal spaceship hovered over Datdaruni, casting an eerie glow over the town. Without hesitation, the girls sprang into action. They gathered their research and rushed to the lab.

As they pieced together their findings, a pattern emerged. The alien technology seemed to be using a blend of electromagnetic frequencies that Kam recognized from her studies. With Phoon's astronomical insights, they realized that these aliens might be using Earth as a staging ground due to its strategic position in the galaxy.

But the breakthrough came from Lita's experiments. She had identified a unique chemical compound that was being used in the alien technology. This discovery unlocked a way to disable the aliens' machinery. The girls knew they had to act quickly.

The next day, they crafted a plan. Using Kam's knowledge of technology, they constructed a device that emitted a counter-frequency to disrupt the aliens' signals. Phoon used her astronomy skills to calculate the optimal time to deploy the device, based on the aliens' movement patterns. Lita synthesized the compound into a liquid form that could be deployed with precision.

As night fell, the spaceship once again appeared over Datdaruni. With their device and liquid compound ready, the girls snuck into the town's central power station. With a rush of excitement and determination, they activated the device, sending out a powerful pulse of electromagnetic interference.

The alien technology faltered, their machines sputtering and failing. In a coordinated effort, Lita sprayed the liquid compound onto key components, causing them to malfunction further. The once-menacing spaceship began to drift, its lights flickering, and it retreated into the night sky.

As dawn broke, the townspeople emerged to find a grateful trio of girls who had saved their town from an otherworldly threat. The news of their bravery and scientific prowess spread like wildfire, making them local heroes.

Phoon, Kam, and Lita returned to Datdaruni School with a newfound sense of accomplishment. Their adventure had solidified their friendship and their passion for science. With the knowledge that the universe held endless mysteries waiting to be unraveled, the girls knew that their journey of exploration was only just beginning.
title: A2F - Datdaruni Science Girls Save the Day

Scene: Two friends, John and Mary, are sitting in a coffee shop.

Mary: I'm not sure. On the one hand, it's a clean and efficient source of energy. On the other hand, there's always the risk of accidents.

John: I agree. Nuclear energy has the potential to be a great source of energy, but it's important to weigh the risks and benefits carefully.

Mary: Exactly. I think we need to invest in research and development to make nuclear energy safer.

John: I think that's a good idea. We also need to make sure that nuclear waste is disposed of properly.

Mary: Right. We don't want to leave a legacy of pollution for future generations.

John: I think we're on the same page. Nuclear energy can be a great source of energy, but we need to be responsible about it.

Mary: I agree. I think it's a promising technology, but we need to make sure we use it wisely.

John: Well, I think that's a good place to end our conversation. Thanks for talking to me about this.

Mary: No problem. It was interesting to hear your thoughts.

John: Me too. I think we both learned a lot.

A debate between two Americans discussing college loan forgiveness:

Person 1: I think student loan forgiveness is a great idea. It would help millions of Americans who are struggling to pay off their student loans. It would also stimulate the economy by freeing up money that people can use to buy homes, cars, and other goods and services.

Person 2: I'm not so sure about that. I think student loan forgiveness would be unfair to people who have already paid off their loans, or who never went to college in the first place. It would also be a big expense for the government, and I'm not sure where that money would come from.

Person 1: I understand your concerns, but I think the benefits of student loan forgiveness outweigh the costs. It would help to close the racial wealth gap, and it would make it easier for people to start their own businesses and create jobs. It would also boost the economy by increasing consumer spending.

Person 2: I still think it's unfair to people who have already paid off their loans. And I'm not sure that student loan forgiveness would actually boost the economy. In fact, some economists believe that it could actually lead to inflation.

Person 1: Well, we'll have to agree to disagree. I think student loan forgiveness is a good idea, and I hope that it happens soon.

Person 2: I'm still not convinced, but I'm willing to listen to more arguments in favor of it.

This is just a brief debate between two Americans discussing college loan forgiveness. There are many other arguments for and against student loan forgiveness, and it's a complex issue with no easy answers.
title: B1D - College Loan Forgiveness Debate

content:
Perry was not like other pirates. He did not like to plunder and pillage, he did not like to drink rum and grog, and he did not like to wear black and red. Perry loved the color pink. He loved pink so much that he painted his ship pink, he wore a pink hat and a pink coat, and he even had a pink parrot named Pinky.
Perry’s crew did not mind his pink obsession. They liked Perry because he was kind and generous, and he always shared his treasure with them. They also liked Pinky because he was funny and smart, and he could imitate any sound he heard.
One day, Perry and his crew were sailing on the pink sea, looking for a new adventure. They spotted a small island with a tall palm tree. Perry decided to explore the island, hoping to find some pink flowers or pink fruits. He took Pinky with him and left his crew on the ship.
As Perry and Pinky walked on the island, they saw a beautiful sight. There was a cave that was filled with pink crystals. The crystals sparkled and shimmered in the sunlight, creating a dazzling display of colors. Perry was amazed by the cave. He had never seen anything so pink and pretty in his life.
He entered the cave, eager to see more of the crystals. He picked up one of them and held it in his hand. It felt warm and smooth, like a precious gem. He smiled and said to Pinky, “This is the best treasure ever! We have to take some of these crystals back to the ship!”
Pinky agreed and said, “Squawk! Pink treasure! Pink treasure!”
They started to collect some of the crystals, putting them in a sack that Perry had brought with him. They did not notice that they were being watched by a pair of yellow eyes from the shadows.
The eyes belonged to a dragon who lived in the cave. The dragon was very proud of his crystals. He had collected them over many years, from different places in the world. He loved his crystals more than anything else. He did not like anyone touching his crystals, especially not a pink pirate and a pink parrot.
The dragon decided to teach them a lesson. He roared loudly and breathed out a stream of fire. The fire hit the wall of the cave, causing some of the crystals to fall down and shatter.
Perry and Pinky heard the roar and saw the fire. They were terrified. They dropped their sack of crystals and ran out of the cave as fast as they could.
The dragon followed them, still roaring and breathing fire. He chased them all the way to the beach, where Perry’s ship was waiting for them.
Perry’s crew saw the dragon and panicked. They grabbed their swords and guns, ready to fight the beast.
But Perry had a different idea. He remembered something that he had read in a book about dragons. He knew that dragons were very intelligent and could understand human speech. He also knew that dragons liked shiny things, especially gold.
He took off his hat and coat, revealing his pink shirt underneath. He also took off his necklace, which was made of gold coins that he had found on one of his voyages.
He waved his shirt and necklace in the air, trying to get the dragon’s attention. “Hey, dragon! Look at this! I have something for you!” he shouted. The dragon stopped roaring and breathing fire. He looked at Perry with curiosity.
“What do you have?” he asked in a deep voice.
“I have some gold for you! It’s shiny and valuable, just like your crystals!” Perry said.
“Gold? Let me see!” the dragon said. He flew closer to Perry, landing on the sand near him. 
Perry showed him his necklace, holding it up to the sun. “See? It’s gold! It’s yours if you want it!” he said.
The dragon examined the necklace carefully. He liked what he saw. He liked how it glittered and glowed in the light. He smiled and said, “Thank you, pink pirate! You are very generous!” He took the necklace from Perry and put it around his neck. 
He looked at Perry again and said, “You can have some of my crystals if you want. They are very pretty, just like your shirt!” He flew back to the cave and brought back a sack full of crystals for Perry. He gave it to him and said, “Here you go! Enjoy your crystals!”
Perry thanked him and took the sack. He looked at Pinky and said, “See? We made a new friend!” Pinky nodded and said, “Squawk! Pink friend! Pink friend!” They hugged each other happily.
The dragon watched them with amusement. He liked them too. He said to them, “You are welcome to visit me anytime you want. I have more crystals to share with you!”
Perry and Pinky smiled and said, “Thank you, dragon! We will come back soon!” They waved goodbye to the dragon and boarded their ship. They sailed away, happy and content. They had found the best treasure ever. A pink friendship.
title: A2F - Perry the Pirate

Emma: Hi, Nong! Nice to meet you from across the world. Well, first off, school here is like this whole big deal. Remember that scene from Harry Potter when they're all sorting into houses? It's kind of like that but with lockers, jocks, nerds, and whatever. 😄

Nong: Haha, I can imagine! We don't have lockers or anything like that. Tell me more!

Emma: So, picture this: you're in the cafeteria, trying to figure out where to sit. There's this unspoken hierarchy of tables, like the cool kids' table, the art club table, and the table where everyone discusses the latest episode of some TV show.

Nong: That's so interesting! What about after school?

Emma: After school, it's all about extracurriculars. Like, some kids are in, like, three different clubs, sports teams, and play an instrument while I'm here struggling to decide which Netflix show to binge-watch next. 🤷‍♀️

Nong: Haha, you're funny! What about weekends?

Emma: Weekends are a mix of adventures and chill time. You know, we hang out at the mall or have movie nights with friends. And there's this phenomenon called "The Weekend Long Nap" that we've perfected over the years.

Nong: Weekend Long Nap? That sounds like a skill!

Emma: Totally is! And speaking of skills, we all have to learn to drive when we're like 16. Picture this: I'm behind the wheel, white-knuckling it, while my dad's in the passenger seat, sweating bullets. 🚗

Nong: Haha, driving sounds scary! How about technology?

Emma: Oh, tech is life here! Social media, memes, and emojis. It's like our second language. If aliens ever invaded, we'd probably try to communicate with them using GIFs.

Nong: That's hilarious! What about food?

Emma: Well, fast food is a religion here. We've got drive-thrus for everything – burgers, tacos, even coffee! But hey, we also have a soft spot for international cuisine. I mean, I can't imagine life without Thai food, right?

Nong: Haha, true! So, is growing up in America all sunshine and rainbows?

Emma: Nah, it's not all rainbows and unicorns. There are awkward phases, embarrassing moments, and times when you just want to escape reality. But it's the imperfections that make it memorable, you know? Plus, we've got the power of memes to help us cope!

Nong: Absolutely, imperfections make life interesting! Thanks for sharing, Emma. It's been really cool learning about growing up in America.

Emma: No problem, Nong! Anytime you want to know more about the land of burgers and memes, hit me up. 🍔😄
title: A2C - Teen Life in America 1

content:
Teen Life in America (according to ChatGPT)
Alex: Hey there! I'm Alex, nice to meet you from Thailand! 🌟

Nong: Hi Alex, I'm Nong. Pleasure to meet you too! So, what's growing up in America like?

Alex: Well, Nong, it's a mix of experiences for sure. School is quite intense here. We've got a lot of focus on academics and getting into good colleges. It's like a never-ending quest for knowledge!

Nong: That sounds serious! What's a typical school day like for you?

Alex: Oh, my days are pretty structured. I wake up early to get a head start on studying. Then it's off to school for classes. We have a bunch of subjects each day, and homework is a constant companion. After school, I usually hit the library or head home to study some more.

Nong: Wow, you really put in the effort! What about extracurricular activities?

Alex: Well, I'm part of the debate club, the science olympiad team, and I play the violin in the school orchestra. It's a lot, but I enjoy challenging myself. Plus, it looks good on college applications!

Nong: That's impressive! Do you have time for hobbies or fun activities?

Alex: Hobbies? I guess you could say my hobbies are studying and learning new things. 😅 But in all seriousness, I do enjoy reading and occasionally going to museums. Oh, and I've recently started trying my hand at coding.

Nong: Coding sounds cool! How do you manage stress with such a packed schedule?

Alex: Good question! It can get overwhelming sometimes. I make sure to schedule breaks to recharge, and I've learned that time management is key. Also, having a supportive group of friends who understand the academic grind helps a lot.

Nong: That makes sense. How about weekends?

Alex: Weekends are a mix of catching up on more studying and squeezing in some relaxation. I try to balance it out by spending time with friends, watching documentaries, and working on personal projects.

Nong: What about the social aspect of school?

Alex: Ah, the social scene. It's not my main focus, but I've found friends who share similar interests. We bond over study sessions, discussing books, and occasionally playing strategy board games.

Nong: Sounds like you've got a tight-knit group! So, is studying everything in America?

Alex: It can sometimes feel that way, especially with the emphasis on academics. But there's more to life than just studying. We're all on our own paths, and for me, diving into books and exploring the world of knowledge is my way of experiencing life to the fullest.

Nong: Thanks for sharing, Alex! It's been really insightful learning about your studious lifestyle and the American education system.

Alex: You're welcome, Nong! Feel free to ask if you ever want to know more about the studious side of life in the U.S. 📚🤓
title: A2C - Teen Life in America 2

content:
Major Milestones When Growing Up In America
Getting a driver's license: This is usually done at the age of 16 or 17, and it gives teenagers more independence and mobility. Driving is also a skill that is often required for many jobs and activities in the US.
Graduating from high school: This is typically done at the age of 18, and it marks the end of compulsory education and the beginning of adulthood. High school graduates can choose to pursue higher education, enter the workforce, or join the military.
Voting for the first time: This is possible at the age of 18, and it allows young adults to participate in the democratic process and express their political views. Voting is also a civic duty and a right that many people fought for in the past.
Turning 21: This is the legal drinking age in the US, and it is often celebrated with friends and family. Turning 21 also means that one can enter bars, casinos, and other places that are restricted to minors.
Getting married: This is a personal choice that varies depending on the individual, but it is usually seen as a sign of commitment and love. The average age of first marriage in the US is 28 for men and 26 for women, according to the Census Bureau.
Having children: This is another personal choice that depends on many factors, but it is often considered a major life event that brings joy and responsibility. The average age of first-time mothers in the US is 26, according to the CDC.
These are some examples of milestones that many Americans experience when growing up, but they are not universal or definitive. Everyone has their own path and pace in life, and there are many other achievements and challenges that can shape one's identity and happiness. What are some milestones that you have reached or look forward to reaching?
title: B1 - Milestones

content:
Movies are a common topic for small talk, but can include a lot specialized vocabulary. Here are some common vocabulary terms related to movies and a few example conversations for different genres:

Vocabulary:

Genre: The category or type of movie, such as action, comedy, drama, sci-fi, horror, romance, fantasy, thriller, and more.

Plot: The main events and storyline of a movie.

Character: The individuals who play roles in a movie's story.

Director: The person responsible for overseeing the creative aspects of a film, including directing the actors and guiding the visual style.

Cinematography: The art of capturing scenes on film, including camera angles, lighting, and composition.

Screenplay: The written script of a movie, including dialogue and stage directions.

Dialogue: The spoken words exchanged between characters in a movie.

Editing: The process of assembling and arranging footage to create the final movie.

Soundtrack: The music and audio effects used in a movie.

Casting: The process of selecting actors for specific roles in a movie.

Example Conversations:

Comedy:
A: Have you seen that new comedy movie everyone's talking about?
B: Oh, you mean the one with the hilarious misunderstandings and witty one-liners? Yeah, I watched it last weekend. Couldn't stop laughing!

Action:
A: Did you catch the latest action flick at the theaters?
B: Yeah, the stunts and explosions were mind-blowing. The main character pulled off some insane feats!

Drama:
A: I just watched this intense drama last night. The performances were so moving.
B: Oh, I love emotional dramas. What was the storyline? Did it leave you in tears?

Sci-Fi:
A: I'm really into science fiction movies. The futuristic concepts are captivating.
B: Me too! Have you seen that new sci-fi film? The special effects were out of this world.

Horror:
A: I can't handle horror movies. They give me nightmares.
B: I get it, they can be pretty terrifying. But some horror fans love the adrenaline rush they provide.

Romance:
A: I'm in the mood for a romantic movie tonight.
B: How about a classic love story or a modern rom-com? Either way, it's sure to be heartwarming.

Fantasy:
A: I'm a huge fan of fantasy films. The magical worlds they create are fascinating.
B: Have you seen the one with dragons and wizards? The filmmakers really brought that fantasy realm to life.

Thriller:
A: Thrillers always keep me on the edge of my seat.
B: I know what you mean. The suspense and unexpected twists in those movies can be so gripping.
title: A2C - Talking About Movies

content:
Once upon a time, there was a little boy named Max who lived in a small village on the edge of a large forest. One day, as he was playing in the garden, he heard a strange noise behind the shed. Curious, he went to investigate and discovered a small, furry creature with big eyes and pointed ears.

Max was surprised and a little scared, but the creature seemed friendly. It made little hops and wagged its tail. Max cautiously reached out his hand and the creature sniffed it. Then it jumped onto his arm and snuggled up to him.

Max was delighted. He had never seen such a strange and cute creature before. He decided to keep it and named it Fips. Every day he played with Fips in the garden and fed it berries and nuts from the forest.

But one day Max noticed that Fips looked sad. It didn’t eat anymore and didn’t want to play. Max was worried and wondered what was wrong. Finally, he decided to take Fips back to the forest, hoping that it would be happier there.

As they reached the edge of the forest, they suddenly heard a loud roar. Max was startled, but Fips jumped from his arm and ran towards the sound. Max followed him and saw Fips run to a large, furry creature that looked like a mix between a bear and a lynx.

The large creature picked up Fips and hugged it. Then it turned to Max and made a deep bow. Max suddenly understood that Fips belonged to this creature and that it had missed him.

He smiled and waved goodbye as Fips and the large creature disappeared into the forest. Max was sad that he had lost Fips, but he was also happy that he had helped him find his family again.

And so ends the story of Max and the strange creature behind his house.

Comprehension questions:

Who is Max?
What does Max find behind his house?
What does the strange creature look like?
How does Max react to the strange creature?
What does Max do with the strange creature?
Why does Max take the strange creature back to the forest?
What happens at the edge of the forest?
What does Max understand at the end of the story?
title: A2F - Max and the Strange Creature

content:
Once upon a time, in a small prairie town, there lived a brave little prairie dog named Pogo. Pogo was known for his courage and quick thinking, and he was loved by all the animals in the town.
One day, a terrible storm hit the town. The wind was howling, and the rain was pouring down. The animals were all huddled together in their burrows, afraid of what might happen.
But Pogo was not afraid. He knew that he had to do something to save his town from danger. So, he put on his raincoat and hat, and set out into the storm.
Pogo bravely made his way through the wind and rain, determined to find a way to protect his town. As he walked, he came across a large tree that had fallen across the river, blocking the water from flowing.
Pogo knew that if the water couldn't flow, it would flood the town and cause even more damage. So, he quickly climbed onto the tree and began to gnaw at it with his sharp teeth.
It was hard work, but Pogo didn't give up. He kept gnawing and gnawing until finally, the tree broke apart and the water began to flow again.
The animals in the town cheered as they saw the water flowing freely once more. They knew that they were safe thanks to Pogo's bravery.
From that day on, Pogo was known as a hero in his town. He had saved them all from danger with his quick thinking and courage. And the animals knew that they could always count on him to be there when they needed him most.
The end.

Qustions
What is the name of the prairie dog who saves his town from danger?
What kind of storm hit the town?
How did the animals in the town react to the storm?
What did Pogo do to save his town from danger?
What was blocking the water from flowing in the river?
How did Pogo remove the obstacle in the river?
How did the animals in the town react to Pogo’s actions?
title: A2F - Pogo Saves the Day

content:
In a bustling schoolyard in Thailand, there lived a charming squirrel named Pip. His fur was as brown as the earth, and he had a knack for grabbing things quickly.
Pip had an unusual friend, a clever crow named Chokdee. Despite their differences, they enjoyed each other's company. Chokdee had shiny black feathers that glistened in the sunlight, setting him apart from Pip.
One day, Pip came up with an idea. He started gathering things like pencils and shiny paperclips from the school grounds and gave them to Chokdee. You see, Chokdee had a fascination for shiny objects. Pip would hide his finds in a tree, which Chokdee called home.
But one day, Pip made a mistake. He took a bracelet from a student's wrist. This made the student sad, and Pip felt terrible for causing that sadness.
Chokdee talked to Pip and explained that taking personal things wasn't a good idea. It could hurt people's feelings. Pip understood and wanted to make things right.
Together, Pip and Chokdee formed a plan to return the bracelet. They used leaves to craft notes and left the bracelet where the teacher could easily spot it. The student was overjoyed to find the bracelet and the notes. She realized that someone cared about making things right.
After that incident, Pip and Chokdee decided to use their talents to spread happiness. They became like heroes around the schoolyard. Whenever someone lost a pencil or other small item, Pip and Chokdee would use their sharp eyes to find them. They even lent a hand in cleaning up the schoolyard, picking up litter to ensure the playground was safe and clean for everyone to enjoy.
As time passed, the schoolyard transformed. In the past children chased off Pip and Chokdee out of fear for their belongings. But now, they laughed with joy whenever the two friends appeared.
title: A2F - Pip Steals a Bracelet

content:
Group Discussion on Online Shopping and Advertising

Moderator: Welcome, everyone, to today's group discussion. Our topic for today revolves around online shopping and advertising strategies. Let's jump right in!

Question 1: Do you buy any products online?

Sarah: Yes, I do. I find it convenient to shop online, especially for items that might not be available locally.

Michael: Same here. Online shopping offers a wider range of choices, and I can compare prices easily.

Emily: I prefer going to physical stores. I like seeing and touching products before buying.

Alex: I buy most things online, but I'm cautious about the credibility of the websites I use.

Jessica: I buy a mix of both. Some items, like clothes, I prefer to try on before purchasing.

Moderator: Great insights! Now, let's move on to the next question.

Question 2: What are some ways companies advertise their products?

Sarah: Companies use social media a lot these days. I see ads on Facebook and Instagram all the time.

Michael: Yeah, and they also use influencer marketing. When a popular person promotes a product, it tends to catch my attention.

Emily: TV and radio ads are still common, especially for bigger brands.

Alex: Don't forget about email marketing. I receive promotional emails from companies I've shopped with before.

Jessica: Search engine ads are everywhere too. Whenever I search for something, related ads pop up.

Moderator: Very diverse methods indeed. Let's move on to the next question.

Question 3: How do ads encourage people to buy?

Sarah: I think emotional appeals work. Ads that evoke feelings make me more interested in the product.

Michael: Limited-time offers and discounts often push me to buy, especially when I think I'm getting a good deal.

Emily: Clear and concise information matters to me. If an ad explains the product well, I'm more likely to consider it.

Alex: Social proof is powerful. When an ad shows positive reviews or how many people use a product, it builds credibility.

Jessica: Personalization gets me. When an ad suggests products based on my past purchases, it feels tailored to my needs.

Moderator: Those are some great points! Lastly, let's discuss the final question.

Question 4: What are some companies or products that are easy to recognize?

Sarah: Apple is instantly recognizable for me. Their logo and minimalist design stand out.

Michael: Coca-Cola's logo is iconic worldwide. You can spot it from a distance.

Emily: McDonald's golden arches are a classic example of easy recognition.

Alex: Nike's "swoosh" symbol is simple yet memorable.

Jessica: Amazon's smile-shaped arrow makes their branding quite distinctive.

Moderator: Wonderful examples! This has been a fantastic discussion. Thank you all for sharing your insights and opinions.

Remember, each person's perspective on online shopping and advertising is unique. This discussion showcased a variety of viewpoints, demonstrating the complexities of consumer behavior and marketing strategies.
title: Learning - Example Group Dicussion (P45)

Once upon a time, there was a scientist named Dr. Elizabeth Smith. She was a brilliant physicist and had dedicated her entire life to unlocking the secrets of the universe. One day, she had a groundbreaking idea: she wanted to build a time machine.

Dr. Smith worked for years on her project. She studied the theories of Newton and other great physicists and eventually developed a machine capable of traveling through time. She called it the “Chronos Machine.”

One day, the time had finally come: Dr. Smith was ready to test her machine. She climbed into the Chronos Machine and set the date: January 1, 1900. Then she pressed the button and suddenly disappeared.

When she reappeared, she found herself in a completely different world. The streets were full of horse-drawn carriages and people in old-fashioned clothes. She had done it! She had landed in the past!

Dr. Smith spent some time exploring the past. She visited famous figures such as Darwin and Florence Nightingale and witnessed historical events up close.

But eventually, she decided it was time to return. She climbed back into her Chronos Machine and returned to the present.

When she returned, she was celebrated as a hero. The world was amazed by her invention and she became famous. But Dr. Smith knew that her work was not yet finished. There was still so much to explore and discover.

And so she continued to work on her Chronos Machine, always in search of new adventures in time.
title: B1F - The Time Machine of Dr. Elizabeth Smith

In a distant future, on a faraway planet, there was a small town known for its famous bakery. The bakery was run by a family who had been baking bread and pastries for generations. The town was located in the western region of the planet, where the landscape was rugged and wild.

One day, strange things began to happen in the town. People started disappearing without a trace, and no one could figure out what was happening. The townspeople were scared and didn't know what to do.

A young girl named Ava, who lived in the town, decided to investigate the mystery. She had always been curious and loved solving puzzles. She started asking questions and gathering clues. She discovered that all the people who had disappeared had one thing in common - they had all visited the bakery on the day they vanished.

Ava decided to go to the bakery to see if she could find any clues. She snuck in after hours and started looking around. In the back room, she found a strange machine that she had never seen before. It looked like some kind of portal.

Suddenly, she heard a noise behind her and turned around to see the baker standing there. He looked surprised to see her and started to explain. He told her that he had discovered the portal by accident and had been using it to travel to other worlds.

But something had gone wrong, and the people who had gone through the portal had not returned. He didn't know what to do and was afraid to tell anyone.

Ava knew that they had to do something to help the missing people. Together, they went through the portal and found themselves on a strange alien world. They searched for the missing townspeople and finally found them trapped in a prison.

With Ava's quick thinking and bravery, they were able to rescue the townspeople and bring them back home. The town celebrated their return, and Ava was hailed as a hero.

After all, it takes courage and determination to solve a mystery and save the day. And sometimes, the most unlikely heroes are the ones who rise to the challenge.

Use your imagination. How do you think Ava rescued the townspeople from the prison? Write down your answer or tell a friend.
title: B1F - The Mystery of the Missing Townspeople

content:
In some states in the United States, it is still legal for teachers to use corporal punishment on students. According to an article from The Economist, corporal punishment in public schools is legal in 19 American states; in private school it is permitted in all but two. In Mississippi, for example, a first-time dress code infraction, public display of affection, repeated tardiness or failure to hand in homework three times in nine weeks makes children eligible for corporal punishment. However, the use of corporal punishment in schools is a controversial issue and there are many people who oppose it.  According to the World Health Organization, around 60% of children aged 2-14 years regularly suffer physical punishment by their parents or other caregivers, and in some countries, almost all students report being physically punished by school staff. Corporal punishment is legally prohibited in schools in 128 countries and allowed in 69 (35%). It is banned in all of Europe and most of South America and East Asia. In comparison, the United States is one of the few developed countries where corporal punishment is still legal in some states. [Source: Bing - Edited]

Corporal punishment is the use of physical force to discipline a child. It can include spanking, paddling, or other forms of physical pain.
Corporal punishment is a controversial issue. Some people believe that it is an effective way to deter bad behavior, while others believe that it is harmful and can have negative consequences.
There is no clear consensus on whether or not corporal punishment is effective. Some studies have found that it can be effective in deterring bad behavior, while others have found that it can have negative consequences such as increased aggression, decreased self-esteem, and difficulty forming relationships.
Ultimately, the decision of whether or not to support the use of corporal punishment is a personal one. There is no right or wrong answer. However, it is important to be informed about the issue and to consider all of the different perspectives before making a decision.
Discussion Questions
Choose a couple of questions to discuss in your group. You won't have time to answer them all. 
What are the benefits and drawbacks of using corporal punishment in schools? Consider the effects on students’ academic performance, behavior, mental health, and human rights.
How do you feel about the fact that corporal punishment is still legal in some states in the United States? Do you think it should be banned or regulated? Why or why not?
How does the culture and history of a country influence its attitude towards corporal punishment? Compare and contrast the views of different countries or regions on this issue.
What are some alternative ways to discipline students without using physical violence? How can teachers, parents, and school administrators promote a positive and respectful learning environment?
If you believe corporal punishment is harmful, how can people raise awareness and educate the public about the harms of corporal punishment? What role can the media, the government, and civil society play in this process?
title: B1D - Debate: Corporal Punishment

Bad poetry is a form of creative expression that deliberately breaks the rules and conventions of traditional poetry. Some people enjoy writing bad poetry as a way of mocking or parodying the genre, while others use it as a way of experimenting with language and imagery without worrying about quality or criticism. Bad poetry can also be a fun and playful way to practice rhyming and word play, as well as to express one's feelings and thoughts in a humorous or absurd manner.
A bad poetry competition is a contest where participants write and share their worst poems, usually based on a given theme or topic. The purpose of such a competition is to have fun, laugh, and appreciate the diversity and creativity of bad poetry. The winner of a bad poetry competition is usually the one who writes the most ridiculous poem, according to the judges or the audience.
Here is an example of a bad poem about a trash can that went missing from our school office.
Oh, where has my dear trash can gone?
It's been missing for so very long.
I miss its scent, its shape, its size,
Its presence brought tears to my eyes.

It was always there to catch my waste,
Never once did it show distaste.
It held my coffee cups and crumbs,
My tissues, wrappers, and chewing gum.

But now it's gone, and I am lost,
My heart is heavy with the cost.
I search the office high and low,
But where it went, I do not know.

Perhaps it's found a better place,
A home where it can show its grace.
But still I hope it will return,
For its absence makes my heart yearn.
title: Learning - Bad Poetry Competition

content:
When people say something is "un-American" or "un-French," they mean it doesn't fit with the usual values, traditions, or ideas of that country. This can cause problems:

1. Exclusivity: It suggests there is only one way to be American or French, which ignores the many different beliefs and values within those countries.

2. Patriotism and Loyalty: It can make people feel like they are not good citizens if they have different opinions or act differently.

3. Suppressing Dissent: It can stop people from speaking out because they are afraid of being called unpatriotic.

4. Stereotyping: It can make people believe in stereotypes about what it means to be from a certain country.

5. Political Manipulation: Politicians may use these labels to make their opponents look bad and gain support for themselves.

In short, calling something un-American or un-French can have a big impact on how people with different opinions are seen and treated. It's better to have open and respectful conversations that include many different points of view.

Can you think of a time that this type of language was used by people or politicians in your country? How did it make you feel?
title: B1 - Labeling as un-American

content:
Idioms are fixed expressions with meanings that are not directly deducible from the individual words they contain. They often carry cultural or contextual significance.

Bite the bullet: To face a difficult or unpleasant situation with courage and determination.

Break a leg: A way to wish someone good luck, often used in the performing arts.

Hit the nail on the head: To describe something exactly or to be precisely right about a situation.

Piece of cake: Something that is very easy to do.

Cost an arm and a leg: Something that is very expensive.

Give someone the benefit of the doubt: To believe someone's statement without being overly skeptical.

Jump on the bandwagon: To join a popular trend or activity.

Spill the beans: To reveal a secret or confidential information.

Turn a blind eye: To ignore or pretend not to notice something.

It's raining cats and dogs: Used to describe heavy rainfall.

A penny for your thoughts: Asking someone what they are thinking or feeling.

A piece of the action: A share in an exciting or profitable activity.

Don't cry over spilled milk: Advising not to worry about something that has already happened and cannot be changed.

The ball is in your court: It's your turn to make a decision or take action.

Once in a blue moon: Referring to something that happens very rarely.

Hit the sack: To go to bed or sleep.

Actions speak louder than words: What you do is more important than what you say.

The apple of my eye: Someone or something that is cherished and loved.

A watched pot never boils: Impatience makes time seem to pass more slowly.

Don't put all your eggs in one basket: Diversify your resources or investments to reduce risk.
title: Learning - Idioms

content:
Before a Test
A: I’m so nervous about the exam tomorrow. I don’t think I studied enough.
B: Don’t worry, you’ve got this. You’ve been working hard and you know the material well. Just relax and do your best.
A: Thanks, that’s very kind of you. I appreciate your support.
B: Anytime. I’m sure you’ll ace it.

Before a Presentation
A: I have to give a presentation in front of the whole class today. I hate public speaking.
B: You’ll do great, trust me. You have a clear voice and a confident posture. Just focus on your topic and don’t mind the audience.
A: But what if I forget what to say or make a mistake?
B: Then just improvise or correct yourself. No one is perfect. The important thing is to show that you care about what you’re saying.
A: You’re right, I guess. Thank you for encouraging me.
B: No problem, I’m here for you.

Before a Date
A: I’m going on a date tonight with someone I met online. I’m so nervous that they won’t like me or that we won’t have anything in common.
B: Don’t be nervous, be excited. You’re going to meet someone new and have a fun time. Just be yourself and show interest in them.
A: But what if they look different from their profile picture or they lie about something?
B: Then you’ll find out soon enough and decide what to do next. But don’t assume the worst. Give them a chance and see how it goes.
A: OK, I’ll try to be optimistic. Thanks for being a good friend.
B: Anytime, buddy. I hope you have a wonderful date.
title: A2C - Giving Encouragement to a Nervous Friend

content:
Once upon a time, in a small town named Cherryville, there was a little girl named Cherry. Cherry was known for her vibrant red hair that matched her name. She was a character full of life and loved to share her joy with everyone around her.
One day, Cherry’s parents gave her an errand to run. They asked her to carry a pair of chairs from her grandparent's house to their home. The chairs were old and worn out, but they held sentimental value as they had been in the family for many years.
On her journey, she came across a pear tree. Hanging from the branches were the most delicious looking pears she had ever seen. She decided to pick a pair of pears to compare their taste with the cherries of Cherryville.
Just as she was about to declare the winner of her little fruit tasting competition, she got a little scare. A bird flew out from the tree, causing Cherry to stumble and fall onto one of the chairs, breaking it further.
Feeling upset about the chair, Cherry decided to take it upon herself to repair it. She went to the library and read books about furniture repair. After days of hard work and determination, not only did she manage to repair the chairs, but she also made them look as good as new.
When Cherry returned home with the repaired chairs, her parents were amazed. They were proud of their daughter’s determination and resourcefulness. From that day forward, whenever anyone in Cherryville needed help with repairs, they knew exactly who to call - Cherry, the chair repair expert!
And so, Cherry found her calling in the most unexpected way. She learned that every task, no matter how small or big, is an opportunity to learn something new and make a difference.
title: A2F - Cherry and the Chair

content:
1. Sarah from New York City, New York: “Dear Penpal, I wish I could bottle up the energy of New York City and send it to you. It’s a city that’s always awake, always moving. I live in a small apartment in Manhattan, surrounded by towering skyscrapers. My favorite place to escape is Central Park, where I often go for a jog or just to read a book under a tree. The city is also a food lover’s paradise - from hot dogs on the street corner to gourmet meals at five-star restaurants, there’s something for everyone.”

2. John from Austin, Texas: “Hey there, friend! Austin is a city that’s as warm and welcoming as the people who live here. We’re known for our live music and let me tell you, there’s nothing like catching a show at one of our local bars. I love spending my weekends at Zilker Park or paddleboarding on Lady Bird Lake. And the food? Barbecue brisket and breakfast tacos are staples here - they’re a must-try if you ever visit.”

3. Emily from Anchorage, Alaska: “Hello Penpal, Anchorage is truly a unique place to call home. Imagine waking up to see moose in your backyard or eagles flying overhead - that’s a regular day here! I love going hiking in the nearby Chugach Mountains or skiing at Alyeska Resort. Despite being a city, it feels like we’re living in the wilderness sometimes. We also have a rich cultural history with many museums dedicated to native Alaskan traditions. It’s a beautiful blend of urban life and wilderness.”
title: A2 - My Hometown 1

content:
Once upon a time, in a small town called Greenfield, there lived a young girl named Lily. She was a curious and adventurous girl who loved animals. One day, she noticed that many pets in her town were disappearing. She was very worried and decided to investigate the matter.
Lily started by asking her neighbors if they had seen any suspicious activity. She also put up posters around the town to raise awareness about the missing pets. However, nobody seemed to have any information about the missing pets.
One day, while walking her dog, Lily saw a man in a van driving around the neighborhood. The man looked suspicious, and Lily noticed that he had a cage in the back of his van. She followed the van and saw that it stopped at an abandoned house on the outskirts of town.
Lily decided to investigate further and went to the abandoned house. She saw that there were many cages inside, and all of them were filled with pets. She realized that this was where all the missing pets had been taken.
Lily quickly called the police, and they arrived at the scene soon after. They arrested the man and rescued all the pets from the abandoned house. The pets were returned to their owners, and Lily was hailed as a hero for solving the mystery of the missing pets.
title: A2F - Lilly Rescues the Pets

content:
Once upon a time, there was a magical tree in the middle of a forest. The tree was very tall and old, and its leaves were golden like sunlight. Under its branches was a spring of clear, fresh water.

One day, a tired traveler came by the tree. He sat down under the tree and drank from the water of the spring. Immediately, he felt refreshed and strengthened. He looked at the tree more closely and noticed that its leaves were glowing golden.

"What a special tree!" thought the traveler. "I must learn more about it."

The traveler asked the tree why its leaves were golden and why the water of the spring was so refreshing. The tree replied: "My leaves are golden because I have the wisdom of centuries stored within me. And the water of the spring is so refreshing because it flows from the river of life."

The traveler was fascinated. He asked the tree to share some of its wisdom with him. The tree said: "I will teach you three things: First, always be kind and helpful. Second, be grateful for what you have. And third, teach others what you know."

The traveler thanked the tree for its wisdom and continued on his way. He followed the tree's advice and lived a happy and fulfilling life.

The magical tree still stands in the forest, sharing its wisdom with those who visit it. If you ever have the chance to see the tree, you should take some time to sit under its branches and drink from the water of the spring. Perhaps it will share some of its wisdom with you, too.
title: A2F - The Magic Tree

content:
Growth Mindset
Imagine you have a magical plant. When you take care of it and give it water and sunlight, it grows and becomes stronger. That's a bit like having a growth mindset.

Having a growth mindset means believing that your abilities and intelligence can grow and improve with effort and practice, just like that plant. It's like saying, "I might not be good at this now, but if I keep trying and learning, I can get better!"

So, instead of thinking you're either "good" or "bad" at something, with a growth mindset, you believe you can get better at anything if you work hard and don't give up. It's a positive way to approach challenges and learning.
title: Learning - Growth Mindest

content:
Types of Intelligence
Imagine intelligence is like a big box of crayons. Each crayon is a different color, and they all help you do different things.

Some people are really good at using the red crayon, which is like being good at math and solving problems. Others are great with the blue crayon, which means they understand their feelings and other people's feelings really well. Some people are like the yellow crayon, good at being creative and making art.

So, there are many types of intelligence, just like there are many colors of crayons. Each type helps us do different things in life, and everyone has their own special mix of crayons that make them unique and smart in their own way.
title: Learning - Types of Intelligence

content:
GRIT
Imagine you have a superpower called "GRIT." Grit is like having a tough and never-give-up attitude, just like a superhero.

When you face a really hard challenge or when things don't go your way, GRIT helps you keep going. It's like having a superpower that says, "I won't give up, even when things are tough!" 

So, having GRIT means you keep trying your best, even when things are difficult, and that can help you achieve your goals and become really strong, just like a superhero.
title: Learning - GRIT

content:
How to Stop Your Smartphone from Controlling Your Life 

Smartphones are amazing devices that can help us communicate, learn, work, and play. But sometimes, they can also take over our lives and make us lose focus on what really matters. If you feel like your smartphone is controlling or distracting you, here are some tips to help you regain control and balance. 

Put educational or productive apps on the home screen 

One way to reduce the temptation to use your smartphone for unproductive purposes is to make it easier to access the apps that can help you learn, work, or achieve your goals. For example, you can put educational apps like Khan Academy, Epic Books, and Google Podcasts on your home screen, so you can quickly access them whenever you have some free time.  

Make less productive apps a few clicks away 

To limit distractions, make less productive apps harder to access by moving them to a different page or folder, or even deleting them. Consider using a second device for these apps to create a physical and mental barrier against impulsive use. This strategy can help you use your smartphone more efficiently and reduce dependence on distracting apps. 

Turn off notifications 

Another way to stop your smartphone from controlling or distracting you is to turn off notifications for the apps that are not essential or urgent. Notifications can interrupt your attention and make you feel compelled to check your phone constantly. By turning them off, you can reduce the noise and focus on what you are doing. You can also use the Do Not Disturb mode or the Focus Mode on your smartphone to block all notifications during certain times of the day or when you are working on something important. 

Use apps that help you monitor your usage 

Finally, you can use apps that help you monitor and limit your screen time. These apps can show you how much time you spend on different apps and categories, such as social media, games, or education. They can also help you set goals and limits for your screen time and remind you when you exceed them. By using these apps, you can become more aware of your smartphone habits and make changes accordingly. 

By following these tips, you can stop your smartphone from controlling or distracting you and use it in a more mindful and beneficial way. Remember that your smartphone is a tool that should serve you, not the other way around.
title: B1 - How to Stop Your Smartphone from Controlling Your Life

content:
Imagine you're trying to draw a beautiful picture, like a unicorn. You want it to be perfect with all the colors and details just right. But you spend so much time trying to make it perfect that you never finish it. You keep erasing and starting over because you're not happy with how it looks.

"The perfect is the enemy of the good" means that sometimes, wanting something to be so perfect can stop you from getting something good or even great done. In this case, you could have a pretty good drawing of a unicorn if you didn't worry too much about perfection. So, it's like saying, "Don't spend forever trying to make it perfect. Just finish it and enjoy your nice unicorn drawing." It's a reminder that it's okay for things to be good instead of perfect, especially when perfect takes too long or is too hard to reach.
title: A2 - The Perfect is the Enemy of the Good

content:
The phrase "The perfect is the enemy of the good" or sometimes expressed as "Don't let the perfect be the enemy of the good" conveys the idea that striving for perfection or an ideal state can sometimes hinder or prevent the completion of a good or satisfactory outcome. In other words, the pursuit of perfection can lead to procrastination, inaction, or an excessive focus on minor details, all of which can prevent progress or achieving something that is already good enough.

This concept is often used to encourage people to take action and make decisions, even if the result may not be flawless. It recognizes that in many situations, it's more productive and beneficial to move forward with a good or adequate solution rather than endlessly seeking perfection, which may never be attainable or practical.

It's important to strike a balance between striving for excellence and recognizing when something is good enough to meet the goals or requirements of a particular situation. This principle is applicable in various aspects of life, including work, creative projects, problem-solving, and decision-making.
title: B1 - The Perfect is the Enemy of the Good
